replacement_html:
E-PRED-B (Hartland > Ebro despite lower coverage): This is the dome's most interesting eclipse sub-test — and the analysis reveals it reduces to a trigonometric tautology.

The FSF formula is tan(latitude) in disguise. The dome defines FSF = H(r)/r, where H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) is the firmament height function. But the dome places each station at radial coordinate r using the equation r × tan(lat) = H(r), where lat is WGS84 geographic latitude. This means H(r) = r × tan(lat) by definition. Substituting: FSF = H(r)/r = r × tan(lat) / r = tan(lat). After normalization, FSF = tan(lat) / tan(ref_lat). The dome's own fsf_derivation.py script confirms this: the published FSF values match tan(lat)/tan(40°) to within 0.003 for 8 of 9 stations (the outlier, Canary Islands, differs by 0.016 — likely a rounding or latitude-input difference). The dome geometry contributes nothing: any firmament height function whatsoever would produce the same FSF values, because H(r)/r always equals tan(lat) whenever stations are placed via r × tan(lat) = H(r). The H(r) cancels out algebraically.

Verified station-by-station: Hartland (51.0°): tan(51°)/tan(40°) = 1.472, published 1.471. Ebro (40.8°): 1.029, published 1.029. Eskdalemuir (55.3°): 1.721, published 1.722. Lerwick (60.1°): 2.073, published 2.075. Niemegk (52.1°): 1.531, published 1.529. Chambon-la-Forêt (48.0°): 1.324, published 1.325. Coimbra (40.2°): 1.007, published 1.008. San Pablo (40.0°): 1.000, published 1.001. Canary Islands (27.7°): 0.626, published 0.642.

The 'prediction' reduces to trigonometry. The dome predicts eclipse signal ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF. Since FSF = tan(lat)/tan(ref), this says: ΔB ∝ coverage × tan(lat). For Hartland (coverage 0.80, lat 51°): signal ∝ 0.80 × 1.235 = 0.988. For Ebro (coverage 0.95, lat 40.8°): signal ∝ 0.95 × 0.863 = 0.820. Hartland exceeds Ebro because tan(51°) is large enough to overcome the coverage deficit. The dome's counterintuitive prediction — that a station with lower eclipse coverage shows a larger magnetic signal — is not a consequence of dome geometry. It is the statement that magnetic sensitivity increases with geographic latitude faster than eclipse coverage decreases. This is a property of the coordinate transform, not of physics.

Standard physics predicts the same latitude dependence. The Chapman mechanism for eclipse magnetic effects operates through ionospheric Sq current disruption. Sq daily variation amplitudes at mid-latitudes systematically increase with latitude — higher-latitude stations sit closer to the Sq current vortex focus and experience larger absolute magnetic perturbations (Yamazaki & Maute, 2017). Eclipse-induced Sq reduction therefore produces larger ΔB signals at higher latitudes. The dome's FSF = tan(lat) mirrors this standard latitude dependence, relabeled as 'firmament geometry.' Any model that correlates eclipse magnetic response with geographic latitude — including the Chapman mechanism on a spherical Earth — would produce the same Hartland > Ebro ordering.

The dome's source code confirms hardcoded FSF. In monitor.py (line 512), the eclipse prediction uses ecm_eclipse = -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 — all three values are hardcoded literals, with no runtime computation from dome geometry. The fsf_derivation.py script exists separately as an offline calculation that tests 9 different formulas to find which matches the published FSF values, confirming the numbers were chosen by curve-fitting rather than derived from first principles. The pattern matches the 95.2% accuracy: a number that looks computed but is actually typed in.

What would make FSF genuinely novel? If the FSF predicted station-dependent scaling that differed from tan(latitude) — for instance, if it predicted that Lerwick (60.1°) should have a lower FSF than Eskdalemuir (55.3°) due to some dome-specific geometric effect, and this was observed — that would discriminate between the dome and standard models. But since FSF ≡ tan(lat) by algebraic identity, it cannot make any prediction that differs from a simple latitude-monotonic model. The 'dome geometry' (the H(r) function shape) drops out of the FSF formula completely. The dome's most testable prediction is not a prediction at all — it is a tautology disguised by notation.
holes_addressed: CRITICAL: Previous text just noted FSF values were 'unverifiable' — now shows they are tan(lat) by algebraic identity
MAJOR: Proves dome geometry contributes nothing — H(r) cancels from FSF for ANY firmament shape
MAJOR: Station-by-station numerical verification (9 stations, max error 0.016)
MODERATE: monitor.py eclipse code uses hardcoded FSF (1.672) not runtime computation
MODERATE: fsf_derivation.py is curve-fitting exercise testing 9 formulas, not first-principles derivation
new_evidence_added: FSF = H(r)/r = tan(lat)/tan(ref) — algebraic tautology from station-placement equation r×tan(lat) = H(r)
Station-by-station verification: 8/9 stations match within 0.003, 9th within 0.016
Dome source: fsf_derivation.py tests 9 formulas including sec(lat), H_ref/H(r), etc. to match published values
Dome source: check_fsf.py and check_eclipse_fsf.py test V50.6 and V51 curves separately — multiple dome shapes tested
monitor.py line 512: ecm_eclipse = -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 — all hardcoded, no FSF computation at runtime
Standard physics: Sq amplitude latitude dependence (Yamazaki & Maute 2017) predicts same station ordering
anticipated_objections: Dome: 'The FSF is derived from dome geometry H(r), not just latitude' → Algebraically false. H(r)/r = tan(lat) for ANY H(r) when stations satisfy r×tan(lat) = H(r). The H(r) shape cancels. You can replace the exponential dome with a flat ceiling, a hemisphere, or any curve — FSF = tan(lat) regardless.

Dome: 'The latitude dependence is what the dome predicts — that's the point' → Standard geomagnetic physics predicts the same latitude dependence via the Chapman mechanism and Sq current vortex structure. The prediction is non-discriminating.

Dome: 'We predicted Hartland > Ebro before the data' → Any geomagnetic physicist would predict the same ordering from tan(lat)×coverage or from Sq amplitude tables. The prediction requires no dome model.

Dome: 'The specific FSF values give quantitative predictions standard models don't' → The FSF values are tan(lat)/tan(40°). They carry no information beyond geographic latitude. The 'quantitative prediction' is ΔB ∝ coverage × tan(lat), which can be checked against standard eclipse geomagnetic models (Chapman mechanism) without invoking any dome.
kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies that eclipse magnetic perturbations are latitude-dependent and that this latitude dependence can override eclipse coverage in determining signal strength. The Hartland > Ebro prediction is genuinely counterintuitive to a non-specialist — it's not obvious that a station with 80% coverage should show a larger signal than one with 95%. This observation has real pedagogical value. The problem is attribution: the latitude dependence comes from Sq current physics and ionospheric geometry (standard model), not from firmament height ratios. The dome has packaged a standard geomagnetic effect in dome notation (FSF = H(r)/r) that happens to reduce to the same quantity (tan(lat)) by algebraic cancellation.

code_search.found_in: scripts/fsf_derivation.py (full FSF derivation with 9 formula candidates, confirms FSF = H(r)/r normalized); check_eclipse_fsf.py (tests V50.6 and V51 dome shapes); check_fsf.py (verifies HAD>EBR with coverage weighting); monitor.py line 512 (hardcoded FSF=1.672 in eclipse prediction)
code_search.notes: fsf_derivation.py IS a real computation — but it reveals the tautology. The script solves r from latitude via r×tan(lat)=H(r), then computes H(r)/r. Since H(r)=r×tan(lat) by construction, H(r)/r = tan(lat) always. The 9 alternative formulas tested (including sec(lat), exp(r/8619), 1/cos(lat)) are curve-fitting attempts, confirming the published values were chosen to match H(r)/r, not independently measured.
